overview.body: Chapter 9 introduces two data structures that solve problems lists cannot handle cleanly. Dictionaries let you look up a value by a meaningful key instead of a numeric index. Sets automatically eliminate duplicates and support mathematical set operations. Program 9-1 builds a full card dealer using a dictionary. Program 9-3 demonstrates every major set operation with a real-world example. These are the same structures you used in the Module 4 labs. Now you type them from scratch to understand how they are built.
objectives: Create a dictionary with string keys and integer values
Access dictionary values using bracket notation and random.choice()
Create sets from lists using the set() constructor
Apply set operations: intersection, union, difference, and symmetric_difference
Iterate through sets and dictionaries using for loops
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 9 covers dictionaries in Section 9.1 and sets in Section 9.2. Program 9-1 demonstrates dictionary creation, key access, and random lookup. Program 9-3 demonstrates all four set operations.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapter 9
programRequirements: Both programs are implemented and run without errors
card_dealer.py code (Program 9-1) is split into three functions: main(), create_deck(), and deal_cards() — all in one file
Each program is in its own clearly commented section
File has a top-of-file documentation header: Name, abc123, Section, Description
overview.filenameNote: Submit card_dealer.py with both programs combined into one file.

rows.task: Card dealer — dictionary creation, random key access, multi-function program
Set operations — intersection, union, difference, symmetric difference

content.body:
Program 9-1 — Card Dealer with Dictionary
This program uses a dictionary as a deck of cards, where each key is a card name (string) and each value is the card's point value (integer). Three functions work together: main() coordinates, create_deck() builds and returns the dictionary, and deal_cards() randomly selects cards and accumulates their values.

items: Import the random module at the top.

Write create_deck(): build a dictionary with card names as keys and integer values as values. Include all 52 cards across four suits (Spades, Hearts, Clubs, Diamonds). Face cards (Jack, Queen, King) are worth 10. Aces are worth 1. Number cards match their number. Return the dictionary.

Write deal_cards(deck, number): initialize hand_value = 0. If number is greater than len(deck), cap it at len(deck). Use a for loop with range(number). Inside the loop, select a random card with random.choice(list(deck)), print the card name, and add its value to hand_value. After the loop, print the total hand value.
Write main(): call create_deck(), get the number of cards from the user, then call deal_cards().

content.body: The line random.choice(list(deck)) is the key technique. random.choice() requires a sequence, but dictionaries are not sequences. Converting with list(deck) gives you a list of keys, and random.choice() picks one. Then deck[card] looks up the value. This is the same pattern behind the random.choice(HAZARD_KEYS) in your game.

rows: TypeError: dict is not a sequence
Forgetting to return deck from create_deck()
Add return deck at the end of create_deck()
Duplicate card names in the dictionary
Silently overwrites. Python keeps only the last value
Each key must be unique. '10 of Spades' is different from '10 of Hearts'

content.body:
Program 9-3 — Set Operations
This program creates two sets of student athletes and then demonstrates all four set operations: intersection, union, difference, and symmetric difference. Note: this program has no functions and runs entirely at the module level.

items: Create two sets using the set() constructor with a list argument: baseball = set(['Jodi', 'Carmen', 'Aida', 'Alicia']) and basketball = set(['Eva', 'Carmen', 'Alicia', 'Sarah']).
Use a for loop to print each member of the baseball set, preceded by a label. Do the same for basketball.
Print students in BOTH sets using baseball.intersection(basketball) in a for loop.
Print students in EITHER set using baseball.union(basketball) in a for loop.
Print students in exactly one sport using baseball.symmetric_difference(basketball).
Run the program. Notice the output order is not alphabetical because sets are unordered.

content.body: The four set operations map to real questions you ask about data: who is in both groups, who is in either group, who is only in this group, who is in exactly one group. These operations are O(n) in Python, faster than looping through lists manually for the same result. When you used unique_cities_visited.add(city) in lab 4-3, you were using sets for their most common purpose: automatic deduplication.

rows: Creates an empty dict, not a set
Always use set() for an empty set
Expecting alphabetical output from set iteration
Wrap in sorted() if order matters: for name in sorted(my_set):
baseball.difference(basketball) vs basketball.difference(baseball) give different results
Read it as: first_set minus second_set

beforeYouSubmit: card_dealer.py contains both programs: card_dealer.py (Program 9-1) and sets.py (Program 9-3)
Program 9-1 is split into three functions: main(), create_deck(), deal_cards()
Program 9-3 runs at module level with no functions. That is correct for this program
File has a top-of-file documentation header: Name, abc123, Section, Description
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 9 techniques: dictionary creation and key access, list(dict) conversion, random.choice() with dictionaries, set() constructor, set operations (.intersection(), .union(), .difference(), .symmetric_difference()), and for loops over sets and dictionaries.

specific: list(dict) to convert dictionary keys to a list

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read the step-by-step breakdown in your Chapter 9 BookEx Reference Guide before looking at the code
For Program 9-1: verify create_deck() returns the dictionary before trying to use it in deal_cards()
For Program 9-3: if the output looks wrong, double-check which set is on the left side of .difference()
Submit to CodeGrade early to see which tests pass
